#!/usr/bin/env bash
#MISE dir="{{cwd}}"
set -euo pipefail

# --- Helpers ----------------------------------------------------------------

info() { printf '\033[0;34m==>\033[0m %s\n' "$1"; }
warn() { printf '\033[0;33mwarning:\033[0m %s\n' "$1"; }
die()  { printf '\033[0;31merror:\033[0m %s\n' "$1" >&2; exit 1; }

write_file() {
  local dest="$1"
  if [[ -f "$dest" ]]; then
    warn "$dest already exists, overwriting"
  fi
  cat > "$dest"
  info "wrote $dest"
}

# --- Phase 1: Preconditions -------------------------------------------------

if [[ ! -d .git ]]; then
  info "initializing git repository"
  git init
fi

# --- Phase 2: Tool installation ---------------------------------------------

info "installing tools"
mise use hk pkl git-cliff

# --- Phase 3: File generation -----------------------------------------------

info "generating config files"

write_file .gitignore <<'GITIGNORE'
# === OS ===
.DS_Store
.vscode/
.idea/
*.swp
*.swo
*.log

# === Security ===
*.pem
*.key
*.crt
secrets/
.vault_pass.txt
.env
.env.*.local

# === Claude/AI ===
.specstory/
.entire/
.claude/*.local.md
.claude/convergence-count
.claude/ralph-activity.log
.cursor/
.cursorindexingignore
.mcp.json
GITIGNORE

write_file .editorconfig <<'EDITORCONFIG'
root = true

[*]
charset = utf-8
end_of_line = lf
insert_final_newline = true
trim_trailing_whitespace = true
indent_style = space
indent_size = 2

[*.py]
indent_size = 4
max_line_length = 100

[*.md]
trim_trailing_whitespace = false
max_line_length = off

[{Makefile,*.mk}]
indent_style = tab
indent_size = 4
EDITORCONFIG

write_file cliff.toml <<'CLIFF'
# git-cliff ~ configuration file
# https://git-cliff.org/docs/configuration


[changelog]
body = """
{% if version %}\
    ## [{{ version | trim_start_matches(pat="v") }}] - {{ timestamp | date(format="%Y-%m-%d") }}
{% else %}\
    ## [unreleased]
{% endif %}\
{% for group, commits in commits | group_by(attribute="group") %}
    ### {{ group | striptags | trim | upper_first }}
    {% for commit in commits %}
        - {% if commit.scope %}*({{ commit.scope }})* {% endif %}\
            {% if commit.breaking %}[**breaking**] {% endif %}\
            {{ commit.message | upper_first }}\
    {% endfor %}
{% endfor %}
"""
trim = true
render_always = true
postprocessors = [
    #{ pattern = '<REPO>', replace = "https://github.com/OWNER/REPO" },
]

[git]
conventional_commits = true
filter_unconventional = true
require_conventional = false
split_commits = false
commit_preprocessors = [
    #{ pattern = '\((\w+\s)?#([0-9]+)\)', replace = "([#${2}](<REPO>/issues/${2}))"},
]
protect_breaking_commits = false
commit_parsers = [
    { message = "^feat", group = "<!-- 0 -->🚀 Features" },
    { message = "^fix", group = "<!-- 1 -->🐛 Bug Fixes" },
    { message = "^doc", group = "<!-- 3 -->📚 Documentation" },
    { message = "^perf", group = "<!-- 4 -->⚡ Performance" },
    { message = "^refactor", group = "<!-- 2 -->🚜 Refactor" },
    { message = "^style", group = "<!-- 5 -->🎨 Styling" },
    { message = "^test", group = "<!-- 6 -->🧪 Testing" },
    { message = "^chore\\(release\\): prepare for", skip = true },
    { message = "^chore\\(deps.*\\)", skip = true },
    { message = "^chore\\(pr\\)", skip = true },
    { message = "^chore\\(pull\\)", skip = true },
    { message = "^chore|^ci", group = "<!-- 7 -->⚙️ Miscellaneous Tasks" },
    { body = ".*security", group = "<!-- 8 -->🛡️ Security" },
    { message = "^revert", group = "<!-- 9 -->◀️ Revert" },
    { message = ".*", group = "<!-- 10 -->💼 Other" },
]
filter_commits = false
link_parsers = []
use_branch_tags = false
topo_order = false
topo_order_commits = true
sort_commits = "oldest"
recurse_submodules = false
CLIFF

write_file renovate.json <<'RENOVATE'
{
  "$schema": "https://docs.renovatebot.com/renovate-schema.json",
  "extends": ["local>basher83/renovate-config"]
}
RENOVATE

# --- Phase 4: hk setup ------------------------------------------------------

info "configuring hk"

HK_VERSION=$(hk --version | grep -oE '[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+' | head -1) \
  || die "could not resolve hk version"

info "resolved hk v${HK_VERSION}"

write_file hk.pkl <<HK_PKL
amends "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v${HK_VERSION}/hk@${HK_VERSION}#/Config.pkl"
import "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v${HK_VERSION}/hk@${HK_VERSION}#/Builtins.pkl"

local base_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["trailing_whitespace"] = Builtins.trailing_whitespace
    ["newlines"] = Builtins.newlines
    ["check_merge_conflict"] = Builtins.check_merge_conflict
    ["detect_private_key"] = Builtins.detect_private_key
}

local commit_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["check_conventional_commit"] = Builtins.check_conventional_commit
}

hooks {
    ["pre-commit"] {
        fix = true
        stash = "git"
        steps = base_steps
    }
    ["commit-msg"] {
        steps = commit_steps
    }
    ["check"] {
        steps = base_steps
    }
    ["fix"] {
        fix = true
        steps = base_steps
    }
}
HK_PKL

hk install --mise

# --- Phase 5: mise integration ----------------------------------------------

info "adding mise tasks"

mise tasks add --description "Run all checks" lint -- hk check
mise tasks add --description "Auto-fix issues" fix -- hk fix
mise tasks add --description "Update changelog" changelog -- git-cliff -o CHANGELOG.md
mise tasks add --description "Full CI gate" --depends lint ci -- echo "CI passed"

info "generating mise scaffolding"
mise generate bootstrap --localize --write
mise generate github-action --write --task=ci

# --- Phase 6: Summary -------------------------------------------------------

info "preset:base complete"
printf '\n  Files created:\n'
printf '    .gitignore\n'
printf '    .editorconfig\n'
printf '    cliff.toml\n'
printf '    renovate.json\n'
printf '    hk.pkl\n'
printf '    .github/workflows/ci.yml\n'
printf '    bin/mise (bootstrap)\n'
printf '\n  Tools pinned:\n'
printf '    hk, git-cliff\n'
printf '\n  Tasks added:\n'
printf '    lint, fix, changelog, ci\n'
printf '\n  Next: run a language preset (e.g., mise preset:uv)\n'

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#MISE dir="{{cwd}}"
#MISE depends=["preset:base"]
set -euo pipefail

# --- Helpers ----------------------------------------------------------------

info() { printf '\033[0;34m==>\033[0m %s\n' "$1"; }
warn() { printf '\033[0;33mwarning:\033[0m %s\n' "$1"; }

write_file() {
  local dest="$1"
  if [[ -f "$dest" ]]; then
    warn "$dest already exists, overwriting"
  fi
  cat > "$dest"
  info "wrote $dest"
}

# --- Phase 1: Tools ---------------------------------------------------------

info "installing Python tools"
mise use python uv ruff shellcheck

# --- Phase 2: Python environment --------------------------------------------

mise config set env._.python.venv.path .venv
mise config set env._.python.venv.create true -t bool

# --- Phase 3: Config files --------------------------------------------------

write_file ruff.toml <<'RUFF'
line-length = 100

[lint]
select = [
    "E",   # pycodestyle errors
    "F",   # pyflakes
    "I",   # isort
    "UP",  # pyupgrade
    "B",   # flake8-bugbear
    "SIM", # flake8-simplify
    "PTH", # flake8-use-pathlib
]
RUFF

# Append Python patterns to .gitignore
info "appending Python patterns to .gitignore"
cat >> .gitignore <<'PYIGNORE'

# === Python ===
__pycache__/
*.py[cod]
*$py.class
.venv/
*.egg-info/
dist/
build/
.pytest_cache/
.mypy_cache/
.ruff_cache/
.coverage
PYIGNORE

# hk.pkl — complete config with base steps + Python steps
info "configuring hk for Python"

HK_VERSION=$(hk --version | grep -oE '[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+' | head -1)

write_file hk.pkl <<HK_PKL
amends "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v${HK_VERSION}/hk@${HK_VERSION}#/Config.pkl"
import "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v${HK_VERSION}/hk@${HK_VERSION}#/Builtins.pkl"

local base_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["trailing_whitespace"] = Builtins.trailing_whitespace
    ["newlines"] = Builtins.newlines
    ["check_merge_conflict"] = Builtins.check_merge_conflict
    ["detect_private_key"] = Builtins.detect_private_key
}

local python_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["ruff"] = Builtins.ruff
    ["ruff_format"] = Builtins.ruff_format
    ["shellcheck"] = Builtins.shellcheck
}

local commit_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["check_conventional_commit"] = Builtins.check_conventional_commit
}

hooks {
    ["pre-commit"] {
        fix = true
        stash = "git"
        steps {
            ...base_steps
            ...python_steps
        }
    }
    ["commit-msg"] {
        steps = commit_steps
    }
    ["check"] {
        steps {
            ...base_steps
            ...python_steps
        }
    }
    ["fix"] {
        fix = true
        steps {
            ...base_steps
            ...python_steps
        }
    }

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#MISE dir="{{cwd}}"
#MISE depends=["preset:base"]
set -euo pipefail

# --- Helpers ----------------------------------------------------------------

info() { printf '\033[0;34m==>\033[0m %s\n' "$1"; }
warn() { printf '\033[0;33mwarning:\033[0m %s\n' "$1"; }

write_file() {
  local dest="$1"
  if [[ -f "$dest" ]]; then
    warn "$dest already exists, overwriting"
  fi
  cat > "$dest"
  info "wrote $dest"
}

# --- Phase 1: Tools ---------------------------------------------------------

info "installing Python tools"
mise use python uv ruff shellcheck

# --- Phase 2: Python environment --------------------------------------------

mise config set env._.python.venv.path .venv
mise config set env._.python.venv.create true -t bool

# --- Phase 3: Config files --------------------------------------------------

write_file ruff.toml <<'RUFF'
line-length = 100

[lint]
select = [
    "E",   # pycodestyle errors
    "F",   # pyflakes
    "I",   # isort
    "UP",  # pyupgrade
    "B",   # flake8-bugbear
    "SIM", # flake8-simplify
    "PTH", # flake8-use-pathlib
]
RUFF

# Append Python patterns to .gitignore
info "appending Python patterns to .gitignore"
cat >> .gitignore <<'PYIGNORE'

# === Python ===
__pycache__/
*.py[cod]
*$py.class
.venv/
*.egg-info/
dist/
build/
.pytest_cache/
.mypy_cache/
.ruff_cache/
.coverage
PYIGNORE

# hk.pkl — complete config with base steps + Python steps
info "configuring hk for Python"

HK_VERSION=$(hk --version | grep -oE '[0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+' | head -1)

write_file hk.pkl <<HK_PKL
amends "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v${HK_VERSION}/hk@${HK_VERSION}#/Config.pkl"
import "package://github.com/jdx/hk/releases/download/v${HK_VERSION}/hk@${HK_VERSION}#/Builtins.pkl"

local base_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["trailing_whitespace"] = Builtins.trailing_whitespace
    ["newlines"] = Builtins.newlines
    ["check_merge_conflict"] = Builtins.check_merge_conflict
    ["detect_private_key"] = Builtins.detect_private_key
}

local python_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["ruff"] = Builtins.ruff
    ["ruff_format"] = Builtins.ruff_format
    ["shellcheck"] = Builtins.shellcheck
}

local commit_steps = new Mapping<String, Step> {
    ["check_conventional_commit"] = Builtins.check_conventional_commit
}

hooks {
    ["pre-commit"] {
        fix = true
        stash = "git"
        steps {
            ...base_steps
            ...python_steps
        }
    }
    ["commit-msg"] {
        steps = commit_steps
    }
    ["check"] {
        steps {
            ...base_steps
            ...python_steps
        }
    }
    ["fix"] {
        fix = true
        steps {
            ...base_steps
            ...python_steps
        }
    }
}
HK_PKL

# --- Phase 4: Tasks ---------------------------------------------------------

info "adding Python tasks"

mise tasks add --description "Install dependencies" sync -- uv sync
mise tasks add --description "Upgrade dependencies" upgrade -- uv lock --upgrade
mise tasks add --description "Run tests" test -- uv run pytest
mise tasks add --description "Type check" typecheck -- uv run mypy src/
mise tasks add --description "Format code" format -- ruff format .
mise tasks add --description "Full CI gate" --depends lint --depends test ci -- echo "CI passed"

# --- Phase 5: Hooks ---------------------------------------------------------

mise config set hooks.postinstall "hk install --mise && uv sync"

# --- Summary ----------------------------------------------------------------

info "preset:uv complete"
printf '\n  Files created:\n'
printf '    ruff.toml\n'
printf '    hk.pkl (updated with Python steps)\n'
printf '\n  Tools added:\n'
printf '    python, uv, ruff, shellcheck\n'
printf '\n  Tasks added:\n'
printf '    sync, upgrade, test, typecheck, format\n'
printf '    ci (updated: depends on lint + test)\n'
printf '\n  .gitignore: Python patterns appended\n'
printf '  Postinstall hook: hk install + uv sync\n'

#!/usr/bin/env bash
#MISE dir="{{cwd}}"

mise use uv
mise use pre-commit
mise config set env._.python.venv.path .venv
mise config set env._.python.venv.create true -t bool
mise tasks add --description "Run pre-commit" lint -- pre-commit run -a
mise tasks add sync -- uv sync
mise tasks add upgrade -- uv lock --upgrade
mise tasks add clean -- rm -rf .venv

[tools]
git-cliff = "latest"
hk = "latest"
pkl = "latest"
python = "latest"
ruff = "latest"
uv = "latest"
shellcheck = "latest"

[tasks.lint]
description = "Run all checks"
run = "hk check"

[tasks.fix]
description = "Auto-fix issues"
run = "hk fix"

[tasks.changelog]
description = "Update changelog"
run = "git-cliff -o CHANGELOG.md"

[tasks.ci]
depends = ["lint", "test"]
description = "Full CI gate"
run = "echo 'CI passed'"

[tasks.sync]
description = "Install dependencies"
run = "uv sync"

[tasks.upgrade]
description = "Upgrade dependencies"
run = "uv lock --upgrade"

[tasks.test]
description = "Run tests"
run = "uv run pytest"

[tasks.typecheck]
description = "Type check"
run = "uv run mypy src/"

[tasks.format]
description = "Format code"
run = "ruff format ."

[env._.python.venv]
path = ".venv"
create = true

[hooks]
postinstall = "hk install --mise && uv sync"

toolUseResult.originalFile:
# repo-forge — Working Plan

## Status

Plugin scaffolded and registered. Mise skill moved in. Marketplace validated.
Two commits on main ahead of origin.

## Completed

- Plugin structure at plugins/devops/repo-forge/
- plugin.json, README.md
- Mise skill with examples and references (python, rust)
- Removed stale `experimental = true` from mise skill
- Added trigger phrases for env vars and layered config
- Registered in marketplace.json, verify-structure passes clean

## Rethinking Scope

Original plan was 5 separate skills: mise, pre-commit, gitignore, git-cliff,
project-setup. After reviewing config-aggregation.md and mise's preset/hook
capabilities, the architecture simplifies.

The real delivery mechanism is mise presets — executable task scripts that
scaffold repos using `mise use` (real version resolution), `mise tasks add`,
and `mise config set`. The skills teach Claude how to use and compose these
presets. The presets do the actual work.

This could collapse to:

1. **mise skill** (expanded) — covers config, tasks, hooks, presets, `mise
   generate`, and the version resolution pattern. The foundational knowledge
   layer.

2. **repo-onboard command or skill** — the "onboard this repo" directive that
   identifies project type and runs the right preset chain. Consumes everything
   the mise skill teaches.

The other originally planned skills (pre-commit, gitignore, git-cliff) become
reference material inside the mise skill or preset logic rather than standalone
skills. Their configs are generated by presets, not hand-written with skill
guidance.

## Key Design Constraints

- NEVER guess version numbers. Use `mise use <tool>` (pins current version at
  scaffold time by querying the registry), `mise ls-remote <tool>` (list
  available), or `mise ls` (what's installed). Training data versions are stale.
- Presets use `mise use` which resolves and pins at scaffold time.
- Mise hooks (enter, postinstall, watch_files) enable runtime validation,
  not just one-shot scaffolding.

## Data Sources

- ~/3I/config-aggregation.md — Phase 2 deep comparison across 35 repos
- ~/.config/mise/tasks/preset/uv — existing preset (minimal, needs expansion)
- https://mise.jdx.dev/mise-cookbook/presets.html — preset docs
- https://mise.jdx.dev/hooks.html — hook events and config
- https://mise.jdx.dev/cli/generate — mise generate subcommands
- DeepWiki jdx/mise — registry, backends, generate internals
- Context7 /websites/mise_jdx_dev — full docs reference

## Research Findings (DeepWiki + Context7)

### `mise generate` — built-in scaffolding

Mise already has generation capabilities we should leverage instead of
reimplementing:

- `mise generate bootstrap` — creates a script that downloads and installs
  mise for contributors who don't have it. Supports `--localize` to sandbox
  mise into .mise/ within the project. Pair with `mise generate task-stubs`
  to let contributors run tasks without global mise.

- `mise generate config` — creates a mise.toml, can import from existing
  .tool-versions files.

- `mise generate git-pre-commit` — generates a git pre-commit hook that runs
  a mise task (e.g., `mise run pre-commit`). Complements the pre-commit
  framework rather than replacing it. Passes staged files via $STAGED.

- `mise generate github-action` — generates a CI workflow that runs `mise run
  ci` on push. Uses jdx/mise-action.

- `mise generate task-docs` — generates documentation for all defined tasks.
  Can inject into existing files or render per-task docs.

- `mise generate task-stubs` — creates shims in bin/ so contributors can run
  `./bin/<task>` without mise installed. Pairs with bootstrap.

Key insight: presets should call `mise generate` where appropriate rather than
writing files from scratch. For example, preset:base should run
`mise generate bootstrap --localize --write` and
`mise generate git-pre-commit --task=pre-commit --write`.

### `mise use` — version resolution lifecycle

1. Parses TOOL@VERSION argument
2. Selects config file (local mise.toml, global, or --path target)
3. Resolves version: checks lockfile first, then queries backend registry
4. Installs if not present
5. Writes pinned version to mise.toml
6. Rebuilds shims

Default is fuzzy pinning (`mise use python@3.13` writes `python = "3.13"`).
Use `--pin` for exact (`python = "3.13.2"`). This is the correct mechanism
for presets — never hardcode versions.

### `mise config set` — programmatic configuration

Can set arbitrary config paths:
- `mise config set tools.python 3.12`
- `mise config set env._.python.venv.path .venv`
- `mise config set env._.python.venv.create true -t bool`
- `mise config set settings.jobs 4`
- Supports --type flag: string, bool, list, set

### Registry and backends

Mise has a three-layer architecture:
1. Registry layer — maps short names to backends (compiled from TOML files)
2. Backend resolution — converts to concrete installers
3. Installation — download, verify, extract, install

Backends: core (node, python, ruby, go, java), aqua (ideal for new tools,
no plugins needed, works on Windows), cargo, npm, pipx, go, github, gitlab,
asdf (legacy), vfox, dotnet, gem, conda, http, s3.

The aqua registry is compiled into the mise binary — no external lookups
needed for tools registered there.

### Task dependency system

Three dependency types:
- `depends` — must complete before task runs
- `wait_for` — soft dependency, waits only if also being run
- `depends_post` — runs after task completes (success or failure)

Dependency args and env can be passed inline:
```toml
[tasks.deploy]
depends = [
  { task = "build", args = ["--release"],
    env = { RUSTFLAGS = "-C opt-level=3" } }
]
```

Tasks support `sources` and `outputs` for incremental execution — skip if
outputs are newer than sources. Useful for build/compile tasks.

### Hooks

Events: enter (once on project entry), cd (every dir change), leave (exit
project), preinstall, postinstall, watch_files.

Can trigger mise tasks directly:
```toml
[hooks]
enter = { task = "setup" }
```

Shell hooks execute in current shell (can source files):
```toml
[hooks.enter]
shell = "bash"
script = "source completions.sh"
```

watch_files monitors patterns and auto-runs:
```toml
[[watch_files]]
patterns = ["src/**/*.rs"]
run = "cargo fmt"
```

postinstall gets $MISE_INSTALLED_TOOLS (JSON array) and per-tool
$MISE_TOOL_NAME, $MISE_TOOL_VERSION, $MISE_TOOL_INSTALL_PATH.

### `hk` — pre-commit replacement (jdx/hk)

hk is a Rust-based git hook manager by the same author as mise. It replaces
the pre-commit framework entirely with significant advantages:

Architecture:
- Read/write file locks enable aggressive parallelism without race conditions
- `check` steps get read locks (run concurrently), `fix` steps get write locks
- `check_first = true` (default): runs check with read lock first, only
  acquires write lock if check fails. Optimizes the common case.
- Uses libgit2 directly instead of shelling out to git (faster)
- Stashes unstaged changes before fix hooks (solves the pre-commit staged/
  unstaged mixing bug)

Configuration: hk.pkl (Apple's PKL language)
- Typed configuration with schema validation
- Builtins for common tools (no version pinning of hook repos needed)
- Also supports TOML/YAML/JSON but builtins only available in PKL

Builtins (built-in linter/formatter definitions):
- JS/TS: prettier, eslint, tsc, biome, deno
- Python: black, ruff, mypy, pylint
- Rust: cargo_fmt, cargo_clippy, rustfmt
- Go: go_fmt, go_imports, golangci_lint, go_sec
- Ruby: rubocop, standard_rb
- Shell: shellcheck, shfmt
- Config: yamllint, yamlfmt, taplo (TOML), jq (JSON), pkl
- Markup: markdown_lint, lychee (link checking)
- Security: detect_private_key
- Git: check_merge_conflict, check_case_conflict, no_commit_to_branch
- Files: trailing_whitespace, newlines, mixed_line_ending, fix_smart_quotes
- Meta: check_conventional_commit, mise (config validation), tombi

Mise integration:
- `hk init --mise` — generates hk.pkl AND mise.toml with hk configured
- `hk install --mise` — hooks use `mise x` for execution
- `HK_MISE=1` env var enables mise integration by default
- Tools managed via mise, hooks managed via hk — clean separation

Migration from pre-commit:
- `hk migrate pre-commit` — reads .pre-commit-config.yaml, generates hk.pkl,
  converts repos to builtins where possible. Preserves hooks, patterns,
  and excludes.

Profiles:
- Steps can have `profiles = List("slow")` to only run in specific contexts
- `HK_PROFILE=slow hk check` or `hk check --slow`
- Useful for separating fast pre-commit checks from slow CI checks

Hook types: pre-commit, pre-push, prepare-commit-msg, commit-msg
Plus `hk check` and `hk fix` as standalone commands (not tied to git hooks).

Impact on repo-forge:
This eliminates the entire pre-commit version drift problem from the
aggregation report. No more .pre-commit-config.yaml with stale hook repo
versions. Builtins resolve tool versions through mise. The migration path
exists (`hk migrate pre-commit`) for existing repos.

## What Presets Need to Generate

### Universal (preset:base)

- `mise use hk git-cliff` (resolved live)
- `mise generate bootstrap --localize --write` — contributor onboarding
- `mise generate github-action --write --task=ci`
- `hk init --mise` — generates hk.pkl with builtins + mise.toml integration
- `hk install --mise` — git hooks use mise for tool resolution
- cliff.toml — canonical template, identical across 13 repos
- .gitignore — composed from modules: os-base, security, claude-ai, plus
  language-specific (python, rust, terraform, ansible, docker)
- .editorconfig — standardized indent/line-length rules
- renovate.json — extend shared config + domain presets
- hk.pkl base steps: trailing_whitespace, newlines, check_merge_conflict,
  detect_private_key, check_conventional_commit
- Standard mise tasks: lint (hk check), fix (hk fix), changelog, ci

### Python (preset:uv, expand existing)

Depends on preset:base.

- `mise use python uv ruff git-cliff rumdl` (all resolved live)
- `mise config set env._.python.venv.path .venv`
- `mise config set env._.python.venv.create true -t bool`
- hk.pkl steps: Builtins.ruff, Builtins.mypy, Builtins.markdown_lint,
  Builtins.yamllint, Builtins.shellcheck
- ruff.toml with baseline rules (E, F, I, UP, B, SIM)
- Standard tasks: lint, format, test, check, sync, upgrade
- Hooks: postinstall (hk install --mise, uv sync)

### Rust (preset:rust, new)

Depends on preset:base.

- `mise use rust cargo-audit git-cliff` (resolved live)
- Cargo.toml [lints] section with clippy warnings
- hk.pkl steps: Builtins.cargo_fmt, Builtins.cargo_clippy,
  Builtins.shellcheck, Builtins.taplo (for Cargo.toml)
- Standard tasks: fmt, lint, build, test, audit, ci

### Terraform (preset:terraform, new)

Depends on preset:base.

- `mise use opentofu tflint terraform-docs yamllint`
- hk.pkl steps: Builtins.yamllint, Builtins.shellcheck, custom tflint step,
  custom terraform-fmt step
- .tflint.hcl, .terraform-docs.yml (standardized from existing 3 repos)
- .yamllint.yml (120-char, 2-space)
- Standard tasks: validate, plan, apply per environment

### Ansible (preset:ansible, new)

Depends on preset:base.

- `mise use ansible-core ansible-lint yamllint`
- hk.pkl steps: Builtins.yamllint, Builtins.shellcheck, custom ansible-lint
  step
- .ansible-lint (production profile, Proxmox-aware skip_list)
- .yamllint.yml
- Standard tasks: lint, test, deploy per inventory

## Migration Path for Existing Repos

For the 19 repos currently using pre-commit:
1. `hk migrate pre-commit` — auto-converts .pre-commit-config.yaml to hk.pkl
2. Review generated hk.pkl, replace custom hooks with builtins where possible
3. `hk install --mise` — set up git hooks
4. Remove .pre-commit-config.yaml
5. `mise rm pre-commit` — remove pre-commit from mise tools

This can be a repo-onboard subcommand or a separate migration command.

## Resolved Decisions

- hk replaces pre-commit. Confirmed. 36 releases in 13 months, zero open
  bugs, same author as mise, built-in migration from pre-commit, builtins
  cover the full stack. hclfmt builtin added v1.36.0 for Terraform.
- PKL is the config format for hk. Builtins require it. The `hk init`
  auto-detection (v1.35.0) minimizes the PKL learning curve — most config
  is generated, not hand-written.
- Presets live in ~/.config/mise/tasks/preset/ (global). They scaffold new
  repos that don't have the plugin yet. The mise skill in repo-forge teaches
  how to build, use, and customize presets.
- Version resolution: always `mise use <tool>` for pinning. Never hardcode.
  Default fuzzy pin (e.g., `python = "3.13"`), use `--pin` only when patch
  version matters.

## Unresolved Decisions

- Line length: 88 vs 100 vs 120. Needs a call. 100 is the compromise.
- Ruff rule baseline: E, F, I, UP minimum. B, SIM, PTH for new projects.
- repo-onboard: skill (auto-activates) vs command (explicit /repo-onboard).
  Leaning command — you want to control when scaffolding happens.
- `mise generate bootstrap --localize` vs non-localized as default.

## Next Steps

1. ~~Expand mise skill~~ — DONE. Hooks, presets, `mise generate`, version
   resolution, hk integration all landed with reference files.
   **Cleanup note:** `examples/examples.md` "Common Base Tasks" section still
   references `prek` and `infisical scan`. Update to hk-based tasks once
   preset:base lands.
2. ~~Build preset:base~~ — DONE. Script at `~/.config/mise/tasks/preset/base`.
   Installs hk + pkl + git-cliff, writes .gitignore, .editorconfig, cliff.toml,
   renovate.json, hk.pkl (version-pinned). Runs `hk install --mise`,
   `mise generate bootstrap --localize`, `mise generate github-action`.
   Adds lint/fix/changelog/ci tasks. Tested: hk check, hk fix, pre-commit hook,
   commit-msg hook all pass.
   **Note:** `mise use` writes `"latest"` for tool versions (no `@version` arg).
   Fine for dev tools — they should float to newest.
3. Expand preset:uv (add ruff, hk steps, mise hooks)
4. Build repo-onboard command (new repos + migration for existing)
5. Build remaining presets (rust, terraform, ansible) iteratively

     5→# Plugin Structure Standards
     6→
     7→## Required Plugin Layout
     8→
     9→Every plugin must follow this structure:
    10→
    11→```text
    12→plugins/<category>/<plugin-name>/
    13→├── .claude-plugin/
    14→│   └── plugin.json          # Plugin metadata (required)
    15→├── skills/                  # AI-powered guidance (optional)
    16→│   └── <skill-name>/
    17→│       └── SKILL.md
    18→├── commands/                # Slash commands (optional)
    19→│   └── <command>.md
    20→├── agents/                  # Subagent definitions (optional)
    21→│   └── <agent>.md
    22→├── hooks/                   # Automation hooks (optional)
    23→│   └── hooks.json
    24→└── README.md                # User documentation (required)
    25→```
    26→
    27→## Plugin Categories
    28→
    29→- `meta/` - Tools for creating Claude Code components
    30→- `infrastructure/` - Infrastructure as Code tools (Terraform, Ansible, Proxmox)
    31→- `devops/` - Container orchestration and DevOps tools (Kubernetes, Docker)
    32→- `homelab/` - Homelab-specific utilities (NetBox, PowerDNS)
    33→
    34→## Plugin Manifest (plugin.json)
    35→
    36→Required fields:
    37→
    38→```json
    39→{
    40→  "name": "plugin-name",
    41→  "version": "0.1.0",
    42→  "description": "Brief description",
    43→  "keywords": ["relevant", "tags"]
    44→}
    45→```
    46→
    47→## Marketplace Registration
    48→
    49→After creating a plugin:
    50→
    51→1. Add entry to `.claude-plugin/marketplace.json`
    52→2. Run `./scripts/verify-structure.py` to validate
    53→
    54→## Creating New Plugins

    57→# Create structure

    59→
    60→# Create plugin.json and README.md
    61→# Add entry to .claude-plugin/marketplace.json

    63→./scripts/verify-structure.py
    64→```

1→---
     2→description: Run pre-commit hooks and create clean, logical commits
     3→allowed-tools: Bash(git:*), Bash(pre-commit:*), Bash(test:*), Task, AskUserQuestion
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Git Commit Workflow
     7→
     8→Orchestrate pre-commit hooks and invoke commit-craft agent for clean, logical commits.
     9→
    10→## Current State
    11→
    12→- Branch and status: !`git status -sb`
    13→- Working directory: !`git status --short`
    14→- Merge/rebase state: !`test -f .git/MERGE_HEAD && echo "MERGE IN PROGRESS" || test -d .git/rebase-merge && echo "REBASE IN PROGRESS" || echo "Clean"`
    15→- Staged files: !`git diff --cached --name-only`
    16→- Sensitive files check: !`git diff --cached --name-only | grep -iE '\.(env|mcp\.json)$|secret|token|key|password' || echo "None detected"`
    17→
    18→## Workflow
    19→
    20→### Step 1: Pre-flight Checks
    21→
    22→Verify repository state:
    23→
    24→1. If merge or rebase in progress, stop and inform user
    25→2. If sensitive files detected, use AskUserQuestion:
    26→   - header: "Sensitive"
    27→   - question: "Sensitive files detected in staged changes. Proceed with commit?"
    28→   - options:
    29→     - Continue (I understand the risk, proceed)
    30→     - Unstage (Remove sensitive files from staging)
    31→     - Abort (Cancel commit workflow)
    32→
    33→### Step 2: Run Pre-commit Hooks (if configured)
    34→
    35→Check if pre-commit is configured: `test -f .pre-commit-config.yaml`
    36→
    37→If configured, execute pre-commit hooks: `pre-commit run -a`
    38→
    39→Analyze the output:
    40→
    41→- If hooks pass: Continue to Step 3
    42→- If hooks fail with errors: Report errors and stop
    43→- If hooks modify files (formatting, etc.):
    44→  1. Show which files were modified
    45→  2. Re-stage modified files: `git add -u`
    46→  3. Continue to Step 3
    47→
    48→### Step 3: Create Commits
    49→
    50→Use the Task tool to invoke the commit-craft agent:
    51→
    52→- subagent_type: "git-workflow:commit-craft"
    53→- prompt: "Create clean, atomic commits for the current workspace changes"
    54→
    55→The commit-craft agent will:
    56→
    57→1. Analyze all workspace changes
    58→2. Group related changes into atomic commits
    59→3. Create conventional commit messages
    60→4. Execute commits handling any additional hook runs
    61→

     2→description: Create and setup a new Claude Agent SDK application (Python)
     3→argument-hint: [project-name]
     4→---
     5→
     6→You are tasked with helping the user create a new Claude Agent SDK application using Python. Follow these steps carefully:
     7→
     8→## Reference Documentation
     9→
    10→Before starting, use the **claude-agent-sdk skill** to ensure you follow SDK best practices:
    11→
    12→```bash
    13→# Load the skill to access patterns and examples
    14→/skill claude-agent-sdk
    15→```
    16→
    17→The skill provides:
    18→
    19→- SDK templates (assets/sdk-template.py)
    20→- Official examples (examples/)
    21→- Best practices and patterns (references/)
    22→- Validation checklist (assets/sdk-validation-checklist.md)
    23→
    24→Also reference the official documentation:
    25→
    26→- Python SDK reference: <https://docs.claude.com/en/api/agent-sdk/python>
    27→
    28→**IMPORTANT**: Always check for and use the latest SDK version (targeting >= 0.1.6).
    29→
    30→## Gather Requirements
    31→
    32→IMPORTANT: Ask these questions one at a time. Wait for the user's response before asking the next question. This makes it easier for the user to respond.
    33→
    34→Ask the questions in this order (skip any that the user has already provided via arguments):
    35→
    36→1. **Project name** (ask first): "What would you like to name your project?"
    37→
    38→   - If $ARGUMENTS is provided, use that as the project name and skip this question
    39→   - Wait for response before continuing
    40→
    41→2. **Agent type** (ask second, but skip if #1 was sufficiently detailed): "What kind of agent are you building? Some examples:
    42→
    43→   - Coding agent (SRE, security review, code review)
    44→   - Orchestrator with subagents (multi-agent workflows)
    45→   - Business agent (customer support, content creation)
    46→   - Custom agent (describe your use case)"
    47→   - Wait for response before continuing
    48→
    49→3. **Starting point** (ask third): "Would you like:
    50→
    51→   - A minimal query() example for simple tasks
    52→   - A ClaudeSDKClient example for multi-turn conversations
    53→   - An orchestrator with subagents
    54→   - Start from the SDK template (assets/sdk-template.py)"
    55→   - Wait for response before continuing
    56→
    57→4. **Project structure** (ask fourth): "What project structure would you prefer:
    58→
    59→   - Single-file uv script (self-contained with `# /// script` header)
    60→   - Standard Python project with pyproject.toml"
    61→   - Wait for response before continuing
    62→
    63→After all questions are answered, proceed to create the setup plan.
    64→
    65→## Setup Plan
    66→
    67→Based on the user's answers, create a plan that includes:
    68→
    69→1. **Project initialization**:
    70→
    71→   - Create project directory (if it doesn't exist)
    72→   - **For uv scripts**: Create single Python file with `# /// script` header including `claude-agent-sdk` dependency
    73→   - **For standard projects**:
    74→     - Initialize with `pyproject.toml`
    75→     - Add `claude-agent-sdk` to dependencies
    76→     - Configure Python version requirements (>= 3.8)
    77→
    78→2. **Check for Latest Versions**:
    79→
    80→   - BEFORE installing, check PyPI for the latest version: <https://pypi.org/project/claude-agent-sdk/>
    81→   - Inform the user which version you're using (targeting >= 0.1.6)
    82→
    83→3. **SDK Installation**:
    84→
    85→   - **For uv scripts**: Dependencies specified in script header, no separate installation needed
    86→   - **For standard projects**:
    87→     - Install with `pip install claude-agent-sdk` or add to pyproject.toml
    88→     - After installation, verify with `pip show claude-agent-sdk`
    89→
    90→4. **Create starter files**:
    91→
    92→   Based on user's choice:
    93→
    94→   - **query() example**: Simple one-shot task (no conversation memory)
    95→   - **ClaudeSDKClient example**: Multi-turn conversation with context
    96→   - **Orchestrator**: Main orchestrator with programmatically registered subagents
    97→   - **SDK template**: Copy from `assets/sdk-template.py` and customize
    98→
    99→   All files should include:
   100→   - Proper imports (`claude_agent_sdk`, `anyio`)
   101→   - SDK best practices (system_prompt, allowed_tools, permission_mode)
   102→   - Error handling
   103→   - Clear comments explaining each part
   104→
   105→5. **Authentication setup**:
   106→
   107→   - Explain that the SDK uses local Claude Code authentication
   108→   - No `.env` files needed when running with Claude Code
   109→   - If running standalone, API key can be set via environment variable
   110→
   111→6. **Optional: Create .claude directory structure**:
   112→   - Offer to create `.claude/agents/` directory for subagent definitions
   113→   - Provide example agent markdown files if building an orchestrator
   114→
   115→## Implementation
   116→
   117→After gathering requirements and getting user confirmation on the plan:
   118→
   119→1. Check for latest SDK version on PyPI
   120→2. Execute the setup steps
   121→3. Create all necessary files based on project structure choice:
   122→   - **Uv script**: Single file with proper script header
   123→   - **Standard project**: pyproject.toml + source files
   124→4. Install dependencies if standard project (uv scripts handle deps automatically)
   125→5. Verify installed versions and inform the user
   126→6. Create a working example based on their starting point:
   127→   - Use patterns from the claude-agent-sdk skill
   128→   - Reference examples from `examples/` directory
   129→   - Follow SDK best practices
   130→7. Add helpful comments explaining SDK concepts:
   131→   - What `ClaudeAgentOptions` does
   132→   - Why `system_prompt="claude_code"` for orchestrators
   133→   - Tool restrictions and permission modes
   134→   - Async runtime choice (anyio vs asyncio)
   135→8. **VERIFY THE CODE WORKS BEFORE FINISHING**:
   136→   - Verify imports are correct
   137→   - Check for syntax errors
   138→   - Validate SDK patterns match documentation
   139→   - Ensure agent names match if using orchestrator
   140→   - **DO NOT consider the setup complete until the code verifies successfully**
   141→
   142→## Verification
   143→
   144→After all files are created and dependencies are installed, launch the **agent-sdk-verifier-py** agent to validate that the Agent SDK application is properly configured and ready for use:
   145→
   146→1. Use the Task tool to launch the `agent-sdk-verifier-py` subagent
   147→2. The agent will check:
   148→   - SDK installation and configuration
   149→   - Proper imports and SDK patterns
   150→   - query() vs ClaudeSDKClient usage
   151→   - Orchestrator requirements (system_prompt, Task tool, agent registration)
   152→   - Permission and security settings
   153→   - Best practices adherence
   154→3. Review the verification report and address any issues before completing setup
   155→
   156→## Getting Started Guide
   157→
   158→Once setup is complete and verified, provide the user with:
   159→
   160→1. **Next steps**:
   161→
   162→   - **For uv scripts**: Run with `uv run <script-name>.py` (automatically handles dependencies)
   163→   - **For standard projects**:
   164→     - Activate virtual environment if needed
   165→     - Run with `python main.py` or `python src/main.py`
   166→   - Authentication uses local Claude Code session (no API key setup needed)
   167→
   168→2. **Useful resources**:
   169→
   170→   - Python SDK reference: <https://docs.claude.com/en/api/agent-sdk/python>
   171→   - claude-agent-sdk skill: `/skill claude-agent-sdk` for patterns and examples
   172→   - Key concepts to explore:
   173→     - System prompts (preset vs custom)
   174→     - Permissions (permission_mode and callbacks)
   175→     - Tools (allowed_tools restrictions)
   176→     - MCP servers (custom tools)
   177→     - Subagents (programmatic registration)
   178→
   179→3. **Common next steps**:
   180→   - Customize the system prompt for your use case
   181→   - Add custom tools via SDK MCP servers (see `examples/mcp_calculator.py`)
   182→   - Configure permission callbacks for fine-grained control
   183→   - Create and register subagents programmatically
   184→   - Review examples in the skill: `examples/hooks.py`, `examples/streaming_mode.py`
   185→   - Validate changes with `assets/sdk-validation-checklist.md`
   186→
   187→## Important Notes
   188→
   189→- **ALWAYS USE LATEST SDK VERSION**: Check PyPI for the latest version (targeting >= 0.1.6)
   190→- **VERIFY CODE WORKS BEFORE FINISHING**:
   191→  - Verify imports are correct
   192→  - Check syntax
   193→  - Validate SDK patterns (query() vs ClaudeSDKClient, system_prompt, tools)
   194→  - Ensure agent names match if using orchestrator
   195→  - Launch agent-sdk-verifier-py for comprehensive validation
   196→  - Do NOT consider the task complete until verification passes
   197→- **USE THE CLAUDE-AGENT-SDK SKILL**: Reference patterns and examples from the skill
   198→- **FOLLOW SDK BEST PRACTICES**:
   199→  - Use `system_prompt="claude_code"` for orchestrators
   200→  - Register agents programmatically via `agents={}`
   201→  - Include `"Task"` in orchestrator's allowed_tools
   202→  - Choose appropriate runtime (anyio.run or asyncio.run)
   203→  - Restrict tools to minimum needed per agent
   204→- Always check if directories/files already exist before creating them
   205→- Ensure all code examples are functional and include proper error handling
   206→- Use patterns compatible with the latest SDK version
   207→- Make the experience interactive and educational
   208→- **ASK QUESTIONS ONE AT A TIME** - Do not ask multiple questions in a single response
   209→
   210→Begin by asking the FIRST requirement question only. Wait for the user's answer before proceeding to the next question.
   211→

1→---
     2→description: Start new ADR - gather context, generate assessment criteria
     3→argument-hint: [decision-topic]
     4→---
     5→
     6→Start an Architecture Decision Record workflow for: **$ARGUMENTS**
     7→
     8→Use the adr-methodology skill for criteria frameworks and templates.
     9→
    10→## Phase 1: Context Gathering
    11→
    12→Ask the user to describe:
    13→
    14→1. **The Problem**: What architectural decision needs to be made? What's driving the need for a decision now?
    15→2. **Constraints**: Timeline, team size, existing technology stack, budget limitations, compliance requirements
    16→3. **Stakeholders**: Who is affected? Who has decision authority? Who needs to be informed?
    17→4. **Initial Options**: Any solutions already under consideration? Prior art or existing patterns?
    18→
    19→Wait for user responses before proceeding. Ask follow-up questions if context is insufficient.
    20→
    21→## Phase 2: Framework Selection
    22→
    23→Based on the context, recommend an assessment framework:
    24→
    25→- **Salesforce Well-Architected** (Trusted/Easy/Adaptable): For enterprise decisions with security, UX, and scale concerns
    26→- **Technical Trade-off** (Operational/Development/Integration): For infrastructure and tooling decisions
    27→- **Custom**: When neither fits, extract 3-5 key decision drivers and create custom criteria
    28→
    29→Explain the recommendation and confirm with user.
    30→
    31→## Phase 3: Criteria Generation
    32→
    33→Generate assessment criteria grouped by the selected framework's pillars. For each criterion:
    34→
    35→- **Name**: Clear, specific identifier
    36→- **Rationale**: Why this criterion matters for THIS decision (not generic)
    37→- **Good looks like**: What success means for this criterion
    38→
    39→Present criteria as a reviewable list. Ask user to:
    40→- Add missing criteria
    41→- Remove irrelevant criteria
    42→- Adjust importance of criteria
    43→
    44→## Phase 4: Save State
    45→
    46→After user confirms criteria, write to `.claude/adr-session.yaml`:
    47→
    48→```yaml
    49→topic: "[decision topic]"
    50→status: "criteria_defined"
    51→framework: "[salesforce|technical|custom]"
    52→criteria:
    53→  - name: "[criterion name]"
    54→    pillar: "[framework pillar]"
    55→    rationale: "[why it matters]"
    56→    good_looks_like: "[definition of success]"
    57→```
    58→
    59→Create the `.claude/` directory if it doesn't exist.
    60→
    61→Confirm state saved and instruct user to run `/adr-assistant:analyze` when ready to evaluate options.
    62→

1→---
     2→name: command-development
     3→description: This skill should be used when the user asks to "create a slash command", "add a command", "write a custom command", "define command arguments", "use command frontmatter", "organize commands", "create command with file references", "interactive command", "use AskUserQuestion in command", or needs guidance on slash command structure, YAML frontmatter fields, dynamic arguments, bash execution in commands, user interaction patterns, or command development best practices for Claude Code.
     4→---
     5→
     6→# Command Development for Claude Code
     7→
     8→> **Reference:** See [command-spec.md](references/command-spec.md) for the official specification.
     9→
    10→## Overview
    11→
    12→Slash commands are frequently-used prompts defined as Markdown files that Claude executes during interactive sessions. Understanding command structure, frontmatter options, and dynamic features enables creating powerful, reusable workflows.
    13→
    14→**Key concepts:**
    15→
    16→- Markdown file format for commands
    17→- YAML frontmatter for configuration
    18→- Dynamic arguments and file references
    19→- Bash execution for context
    20→- Command organization and namespacing
    21→
    22→## Command Basics
    23→
    24→### What is a Slash Command?
    25→
    26→A slash command is a Markdown file containing a prompt that Claude executes when invoked. Commands provide:
    27→
    28→- **Reusability**: Define once, use repeatedly
    29→- **Consistency**: Standardize common workflows
    30→- **Sharing**: Distribute across team or projects
    31→- **Efficiency**: Quick access to complex prompts
    32→
    33→### Critical: Commands are Instructions FOR Claude
    34→
    35→**Commands are written for agent consumption, not human consumption.**
    36→
    37→When a user invokes `/command-name`, the command content becomes Claude's instructions. Write commands as directives TO Claude about what to do, not as messages TO the user.
    38→
    39→**Correct approach (instructions for Claude):**
    40→
    41→```markdown
    42→Review this code for security vulnerabilities including:
    43→- SQL injection
    44→- XSS attacks
    45→- Authentication issues
    46→
    47→Provide specific line numbers and severity ratings.
    48→```
    49→
    50→**Incorrect approach (messages to user):**
    51→
    52→```markdown
    53→This command will review your code for security issues.
    54→You'll receive a report with vulnerability details.
    55→```
    56→
    57→The first example tells Claude what to do. The second tells the user what will happen but doesn't instruct Claude. Always use the first approach.
    58→
    59→### Command Locations
    60→
    61→**Project commands** (shared with team):
    62→
    63→- Location: `.claude/commands/`
    64→- Scope: Available in specific project
    65→- Label: Shown as "(project)" in `/help`
    66→- Use for: Team workflows, project-specific tasks
    67→
    68→**Personal commands** (available everywhere):
    69→
    70→- Location: `~/.claude/commands/`
    71→- Scope: Available in all projects
    72→- Label: Shown as "(user)" in `/help`
    73→- Use for: Personal workflows, cross-project utilities
    74→
    75→**Plugin commands** (bundled with plugins):
    76→
    77→- Location: `plugin-name/commands/`
    78→- Scope: Available when plugin installed
    79→- Label: Shown as "(plugin-name)" in `/help`
    80→- Use for: Plugin-specific functionality
    81→
    82→## File Format
    83→
    84→### Basic Structure
    85→
    86→Commands are Markdown files with `.md` extension:
    87→
    88→```text
    89→.claude/commands/
    90→├── review.md           # /review command
    91→├── test.md             # /test command
    92→└── deploy.md           # /deploy command
    93→```
    94→
    95→**Simple command:**
    96→
    97→```markdown
    98→Review this code for security vulnerabilities including:
    99→- SQL injection
   100→- XSS attacks
   101→- Authentication bypass
   102→- Insecure data handling
   103→```
   104→
   105→No frontmatter needed for basic commands.
   106→
   107→### With YAML Frontmatter
   108→
   109→Add configuration using YAML frontmatter:
   110→
   111→```markdown
   112→---
   113→description: Review code for security issues
   114→allowed-tools: Read, Grep, Bash(git:*)
   115→model: sonnet
   116→---
   117→
   118→Review this code for security vulnerabilities...
   119→```
   120→
   121→## YAML Frontmatter Fields
   122→
   123→### description
   124→
   125→**Purpose:** Brief description shown in `/help`
   126→**Type:** String
   127→**Default:** First line of command prompt
   128→
   129→```yaml
   130→---
   131→description: Review pull request for code quality
   132→---
   133→```
   134→
   135→**Best practice:** Clear, actionable description (under 60 characters)
   136→
   137→### allowed-tools
   138→
   139→**Purpose:** Specify which tools command can use
   140→**Type:** Comma-separated string
   141→**Default:** Inherits from conversation
   142→
   143→```yaml
   144→---
   145→allowed-tools: Read, Write, Edit, Bash(git:*)
   146→---
   147→```
   148→
   149→**Note:** Always use comma-separated format on a single line. Do not use YAML array syntax.
   150→
   151→**Patterns:**
   152→
   153→- `Read, Write, Edit` - Specific tools
   154→- `Bash(git:*)` - Bash with git commands only
   155→- `*` - All tools (rarely needed)
   156→
   157→**Use when:** Command requires specific tool access
   158→
   159→### model
   160→
   161→**Purpose:** Specify model for command execution
   162→**Type:** String (sonnet, opus, haiku)
   163→**Default:** Inherits from conversation
   164→
   165→```yaml
   166→---
   167→model: haiku
   168→---
   169→```
   170→
   171→**Use cases:**
   172→
   173→- `haiku` - Fast, simple commands
   174→- `sonnet` - Standard workflows
   175→- `opus` - Complex analysis
   176→
   177→### argument-hint
   178→
   179→**Purpose:** Document expected arguments for autocomplete
   180→**Type:** String
   181→**Default:** None
   182→
   183→```yaml
   184→---
   185→argument-hint: [pr-number] [priority] [assignee]
   186→---
   187→```
   188→
   189→**Benefits:**
   190→
   191→- Helps users understand command arguments
   192→- Improves command discovery
   193→- Documents command interface
   194→
   195→### disable-model-invocation
   196→
   197→**Purpose:** Prevent SlashCommand tool from programmatically calling command
   198→**Type:** Boolean
   199→**Default:** false
   200→
   201→```yaml
   202→---
   203→disable-model-invocation: true
   204→---
   205→```
   206→
   207→**Use when:** Command should only be manually invoked
   208→
   209→## Dynamic Arguments
   210→
   211→### Using $ARGUMENTS
   212→
   213→Capture all arguments as single string:
   214→
   215→```markdown
   216→---
   217→description: Fix issue by number
   218→argument-hint: [issue-number]
   219→---
   220→
   221→Fix issue #$ARGUMENTS following our coding standards and best practices.
   222→```
   223→
   224→**Usage:**
   225→
   226→```text
   227→> /fix-issue 123
   228→> /fix-issue 456
   229→```
   230→
   231→**Expands to:**
   232→
   233→```text
   234→Fix issue #123 following our coding standards...
   235→Fix issue #456 following our coding standards...
   236→```
   237→
   238→### Using Positional Arguments
   239→
   240→Capture individual arguments with `$1`, `$2`, `$3`, etc.:
   241→
   242→```markdown
   243→---
   244→description: Review PR with priority and assignee
   245→argument-hint: [pr-number] [priority] [assignee]
   246→---
   247→
   248→Review pull request #$1 with priority level $2.
   249→After review, assign to $3 for follow-up.
   250→```
   251→
   252→**Usage:**
   253→
   254→```text
   255→> /review-pr 123 high alice
   256→```
   257→
   258→**Expands to:**
   259→
   260→```bash
   261→Review pull request #123 with priority level high.
   262→After review, assign to alice for follow-up.
   263→```
   264→
   265→### Combining Arguments
   266→
   267→Mix positional and remaining arguments:
   268→
   269→```markdown
   270→Deploy $1 to $2 environment with options: $3
   271→```
   272→
   273→**Usage:**
   274→
   275→```text
   276→> /deploy api staging --force --skip-tests
   277→```
   278→
   279→**Expands to:**
   280→
   281→```text
   282→Deploy api to staging environment with options: --force --skip-tests
   283→```
   284→
   285→## File References
   286→
   287→### Using @ Syntax
   288→
   289→Include file contents in command:
   290→
   291→```markdown
   292→---
   293→description: Review specific file
   294→argument-hint: [file-path]
   295→---
   296→
   297→Review @$1 for:
   298→- Code quality
   299→- Best practices
   300→- Potential bugs
   301→```
   302→
   303→**Usage:**
   304→
   305→```text
   306→> /review-file src/api/users.ts
   307→```
   308→
   309→**Effect:** Claude reads `src/api/users.ts` before processing command
   310→
   311→### Multiple File References
   312→
   313→Reference multiple files:
   314→
   315→```markdown
   316→Compare @src/old-version.js with @src/new-version.js
   317→
   318→Identify:
   319→- Breaking changes
   320→- New features
   321→- Bug fixes
   322→```
   323→
   324→### Static File References
   325→
   326→Reference known files without arguments:
   327→
   328→```markdown
   329→Review @package.json and @tsconfig.json for consistency
   330→
   331→Ensure:
   332→- TypeScript version matches
   333→- Dependencies are aligned
   334→- Build configuration is correct
   335→```
   336→
   337→## Bash Execution in Commands
   338→
   339→Commands can execute bash commands inline to dynamically gather context before Claude processes the command. This is useful for including repository state, environment information, or project-specific context.
   340→
   341→**When to use:**
   342→
   343→- Include dynamic context (git status, environment vars, etc.)
   344→- Gather project/repository state
   345→- Build context-aware workflows
   346→
   347→**Implementation details:**
   348→For complete syntax, examples, and best practices, see `references/plugin-features-reference.md` section on bash execution. The reference includes the exact syntax and multiple working examples to avoid execution issues
   349→
   350→## Command Organization
   351→
   352→### Flat Structure
   353→
   354→Simple organization for small command sets:
   355→
   356→```text
   357→.claude/commands/
   358→├── build.md
   359→├── test.md
   360→├── deploy.md
   361→├── review.md
   362→└── docs.md
   363→```
   364→
   365→**Use when:** 5-15 commands, no clear categories
   366→
   367→### Namespaced Structure
   368→
   369→Organize commands in subdirectories:
   370→
   371→```text
   372→.claude/commands/
   373→├── ci/
   374→│   ├── build.md        # /build (project:ci)
   375→│   ├── test.md         # /test (project:ci)
   376→│   └── lint.md         # /lint (project:ci)
   377→├── git/
   378→│   ├── commit.md       # /commit (project:git)
   379→│   └── pr.md           # /pr (project:git)
   380→└── docs/
   381→    ├── generate.md     # /generate (project:docs)
   382→    └── publish.md      # /publish (project:docs)
   383→```
   384→
   385→**Benefits:**
   386→
   387→- Logical grouping by category
   388→- Namespace shown in `/help`
   389→- Easier to find related commands
   390→
   391→**Use when:** 15+ commands, clear categories
   392→
   393→## Best Practices
   394→
   395→### Command Design
   396→
   397→1. **Single responsibility:** One command, one task
   398→2. **Clear descriptions:** Self-explanatory in `/help`
   399→3. **Explicit dependencies:** Use `allowed-tools` when needed
   400→4. **Document arguments:** Always provide `argument-hint`
   401→5. **Consistent naming:** Use verb-noun pattern (review-pr, fix-issue)
   402→
   403→### Argument Handling
   404→
   405→1. **Validate arguments:** Check for required arguments in prompt
   406→2. **Provide defaults:** Suggest defaults when arguments missing
   407→3. **Document format:** Explain expected argument format
   408→4. **Handle edge cases:** Consider missing or invalid arguments
   409→
   410→```markdown
   411→---
   412→argument-hint: [pr-number]
   413→---
   414→
   415→$IF($1,
   416→  Review PR #$1,
   417→  Please provide a PR number. Usage: /review-pr [number]
   418→)
   419→```
   420→
   421→### File References
   422→
   423→1. **Explicit paths:** Use clear file paths
   424→2. **Check existence:** Handle missing files gracefully
   425→3. **Relative paths:** Use project-relative paths
   426→4. **Glob support:** Consider using Glob tool for patterns
   427→
   428→### Bash Commands
   429→
   430→1. **Limit scope:** Use `Bash(git:*)` not `Bash(*)`
   431→2. **Safe commands:** Avoid destructive operations
   432→3. **Handle errors:** Consider command failures
   433→4. **Keep fast:** Long-running commands slow invocation
   434→
   435→### Documentation
   436→
   437→1. **Add comments:** Explain complex logic
   438→2. **Provide examples:** Show usage in comments
   439→3. **List requirements:** Document dependencies
   440→4. **Version commands:** Note breaking changes
   441→
   442→```markdown
   443→---
   444→description: Deploy application to environment
   445→argument-hint: [environment] [version]
   446→---
   447→
   448→<!--
   449→Usage: /deploy [staging|production] [version]
   450→Requires: AWS credentials configured
   451→Example: /deploy staging v1.2.3
   452→-->
   453→
   454→Deploy application to $1 environment using version $2...
   455→```
   456→
   457→## Common Patterns
   458→
   459→### Review Pattern
   460→
   461→```markdown
   462→---
   463→description: Review code changes
   464→allowed-tools: Read, Bash(git:*)
   465→---
   466→
   467→Files changed: $ git diff --name-only
   468→
   469→Review each file for:
   470→1. Code quality and style
   471→2. Potential bugs or issues
   472→3. Test coverage
   473→4. Documentation needs
   474→
   475→Provide specific feedback for each file.
   476→```
   477→
   478→### Testing Pattern
   479→
   480→```markdown
   481→---
   482→description: Run tests for specific file
   483→argument-hint: [test-file]
   484→allowed-tools: Bash(npm:*)
   485→---
   486→
   487→Run tests: $ npm test $1
   488→
   489→Analyze results and suggest fixes for failures.
   490→```
   491→
   492→### Documentation Pattern
   493→
   494→```markdown
   495→---
   496→description: Generate documentation for file
   497→argument-hint: [source-file]
   498→---
   499→
   500→Generate comprehensive documentation for @$1 including:
   501→- Function/class descriptions
   502→- Parameter documentation
   503→- Return value descriptions
   504→- Usage examples
   505→- Edge cases and errors
   506→```
   507→
   508→### Workflow Pattern
   509→
   510→```markdown
   511→---
   512→description: Complete PR workflow
   513→argument-hint: [pr-number]
   514→allowed-tools: Bash(gh:*), Read
   515→---
   516→
   517→PR #$1 Workflow:
   518→
   519→1. Fetch PR: Run `gh pr view $1`
   520→2. Review changes
   521→3. Run checks
   522→4. Approve or request changes
   523→```
   524→
   525→## Troubleshooting
   526→
   527→**Command not appearing:**
   528→
   529→- Check file is in correct directory
   530→- Verify `.md` extension present
   531→- Ensure valid Markdown format
   532→- Restart Claude Code
   533→
   534→**Arguments not working:**
   535→
   536→- Verify `$1`, `$2` syntax correct
   537→- Check `argument-hint` matches usage
   538→- Ensure no extra spaces
   539→
   540→**Bash execution failing:**
   541→
   542→- Check `allowed-tools` includes Bash
   543→- Verify command syntax in backticks
   544→- Test command in terminal first
   545→- Check for required permissions
   546→
   547→**File references not working:**
   548→
   549→- Verify `@` syntax correct
   550→- Check file path is valid
   551→- Ensure Read tool allowed
   552→- Use absolute or project-relative paths
   553→
   554→## Plugin-Specific Features
   555→
   556→### CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT Variable
   557→
   558→Plugin commands have access to `${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}`, an environment variable that resolves to the plugin's absolute path.
   559→
   560→**Purpose:**
   561→
   562→- Reference plugin files portably
   563→- Execute plugin scripts
   564→- Load plugin configuration
   565→- Access plugin templates
   566→
   567→**Basic usage:**
   568→
   569→```markdown
   570→---
   571→description: Analyze using plugin script
   572→allowed-tools: Bash(node:*)
   573→---
   574→
   575→Run analysis: $ node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/analyze.js $1
   576→
   577→Review results and report findings.
   578→```
   579→
   580→**Common patterns:**
   581→
   582→```markdown
   583→# Execute plugin script
   584→$ bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/script.sh
   585→
   586→# Load plugin configuration
   587→@${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/config/settings.json
   588→
   589→# Use plugin template
   590→@${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/templates/report.md
   591→
   592→# Access plugin resources
   593→@${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/docs/reference.md
   594→```
   595→
   596→**Why use it:**
   597→
   598→- Works across all installations
   599→- Portable between systems
   600→- No hardcoded paths needed
   601→- Essential for multi-file plugins
   602→
   603→### Plugin Command Organization
   604→
   605→Plugin commands discovered automatically from `commands/` directory:
   606→
   607→```text
   608→plugin-name/
   609→├── commands/
   610→│   ├── foo.md              # /foo (plugin:plugin-name)
   611→│   ├── bar.md              # /bar (plugin:plugin-name)
   612→│   └── utils/
   613→│       └── helper.md       # /helper (plugin:plugin-name:utils)
   614→└── plugin.json
   615→```
   616→
   617→**Namespace benefits:**
   618→
   619→- Logical command grouping
   620→- Shown in `/help` output
   621→- Avoid name conflicts
   622→- Organize related commands
   623→
   624→**Naming conventions:**
   625→
   626→- Use descriptive action names
   627→- Avoid generic names (test, run)
   628→- Consider plugin-specific prefix
   629→- Use hyphens for multi-word names
   630→
   631→### Plugin Command Patterns
   632→
   633→**Configuration-based pattern:**
   634→
   635→```markdown
   636→---
   637→description: Deploy using plugin configuration
   638→argument-hint: [environment]
   639→allowed-tools: Read, Bash(*)
   640→---
   641→
   642→Load configuration: @${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/config/$1-deploy.json
   643→
   644→Deploy to $1 using configuration settings.
   645→Monitor deployment and report status.
   646→```
   647→
   648→**Template-based pattern:**
   649→
   650→```markdown
   651→---
   652→description: Generate docs from template
   653→argument-hint: [component]
   654→---
   655→
   656→Template: @${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/templates/docs.md
   657→
   658→Generate documentation for $1 following template structure.
   659→```
   660→
   661→**Multi-script pattern:**
   662→
   663→```markdown
   664→---
   665→description: Complete build workflow
   666→allowed-tools: Bash(*)
   667→---
   668→
   669→Build: $ bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/build.sh
   670→Test: $ bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/test.sh
   671→Package: $ bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/package.sh
   672→
   673→Review outputs and report workflow status.
   674→```
   675→
   676→**See `references/plugin-features-reference.md` for detailed patterns.**
   677→
   678→## Integration with Plugin Components
   679→
   680→Commands can integrate with other plugin components for powerful workflows.
   681→
   682→### Agent Integration
   683→
   684→Launch plugin agents for complex tasks:
   685→
   686→```markdown
   687→---
   688→description: Deep code review
   689→argument-hint: [file-path]
   690→---
   691→
   692→Initiate comprehensive review of @$1 using the code-reviewer agent.
   693→
   694→The agent will analyze:
   695→- Code structure
   696→- Security issues
   697→- Performance
   698→- Best practices
   699→
   700→Agent uses plugin resources:
   701→- ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/config/rules.json
   702→- ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/checklists/review.md
   703→```
   704→
   705→**Key points:**
   706→
   707→- Agent must exist in `plugin/agents/` directory
   708→- Claude uses Task tool to launch agent
   709→- Document agent capabilities
   710→- Reference plugin resources agent uses
   711→
   712→### Skill Integration
   713→
   714→Leverage plugin skills for specialized knowledge:
   715→
   716→```markdown
   717→---
   718→description: Document API with standards
   719→argument-hint: [api-file]
   720→---
   721→
   722→Document API in @$1 following plugin standards.
   723→
   724→Use the api-docs-standards skill to ensure:
   725→- Complete endpoint documentation
   726→- Consistent formatting
   727→- Example quality
   728→- Error documentation
   729→
   730→Generate production-ready API docs.
   731→```
   732→
   733→**Key points:**
   734→
   735→- Skill must exist in `plugin/skills/` directory
   736→- Mention skill name to trigger invocation
   737→- Document skill purpose
   738→- Explain what skill provides
   739→
   740→### Hook Coordination
   741→
   742→Design commands that work with plugin hooks:
   743→
   744→- Commands can prepare state for hooks to process
   745→- Hooks execute automatically on tool events
   746→- Commands should document expected hook behavior
   747→- Guide Claude on interpreting hook output
   748→
   749→See `references/plugin-features-reference.md` for examples of commands that coordinate with hooks
   750→
   751→### Multi-Component Workflows
   752→
   753→Combine agents, skills, and scripts:
   754→
   755→```markdown
   756→---
   757→description: Comprehensive review workflow
   758→argument-hint: [file]
   759→allowed-tools: Bash(node:*), Read
   760→---
   761→
   762→Target: @$1
   763→
   764→Phase 1 - Static Analysis:
   765→$ node ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/lint.js $1
   766→
   767→Phase 2 - Deep Review:
   768→Launch code-reviewer agent for detailed analysis.
   769→
   770→Phase 3 - Standards Check:
   771→Use coding-standards skill for validation.
   772→
   773→Phase 4 - Report:
   774→Template: @${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/templates/review.md
   775→
   776→Compile findings into report following template.
   777→```
   778→
   779→**When to use:**
   780→
   781→- Complex multi-step workflows
   782→- Leverage multiple plugin capabilities
   783→- Require specialized analysis
   784→- Need structured outputs
   785→
   786→## Validation Patterns
   787→
   788→Commands should validate inputs and resources before processing.
   789→
   790→### Argument Validation
   791→
   792→```markdown
   793→---
   794→description: Deploy with validation
   795→argument-hint: [environment]
   796→---
   797→
   798→Validate environment: $ echo "$1" | grep -E "^(dev|staging|prod)$" || echo "INVALID"
   799→
   800→If $1 is valid environment:
   801→  Deploy to $1
   802→Otherwise:
   803→  Explain valid environments: dev, staging, prod
   804→  Show usage: /deploy [environment]
   805→```
   806→
   807→### File Existence Checks
   808→
   809→```markdown
   810→---
   811→description: Process configuration
   812→argument-hint: [config-file]
   813→---
   814→
   815→Check file exists: $ test -f $1 && echo "EXISTS" || echo "MISSING"
   816→
   817→If file exists:
   818→  Process configuration: @$1
   819→Otherwise:
   820→  Explain where to place config file
   821→  Show expected format
   822→  Provide example configuration
   823→```
   824→
   825→### Plugin Resource Validation
   826→
   827→```markdown
   828→---
   829→description: Run plugin analyzer
   830→allowed-tools: Bash(test:*)
   831→---
   832→
   833→Validate plugin setup:
   834→- Script: $ test -x ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/bin/analyze && echo "✓" || echo "✗"
   835→- Config: $ test -f ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/config.json && echo "✓" || echo "✗"
   836→
   837→If all checks pass, run analysis.
   838→Otherwise, report missing components.
   839→```
   840→
   841→### Error Handling
   842→
   843→```markdown
   844→---
   845→description: Build with error handling
   846→allowed-tools: Bash(*)
   847→---
   848→
   849→Execute build: $ bash ${CLAUDE_PLUGIN_ROOT}/scripts/build.sh 2>&1 || echo "BUILD_FAILED"
   850→
   851→If build succeeded:
   852→  Report success and output location
   853→If build failed:
   854→  Analyze error output
   855→  Suggest likely causes
   856→  Provide troubleshooting steps
   857→```
   858→
   859→**Best practices:**
   860→
   861→- Validate early in command
   862→- Provide helpful error messages
   863→- Suggest corrective actions
   864→- Handle edge cases gracefully
   865→
